driver.body: The final driver is the ten-year question that hangs over every charging asset the domain will ever build: does Energy's **cost floor and trust ceiling survive a decarbonising, re-pricing grid** [S-031]? Alone among the six domains, its core input — the wholesale price of electricity — is set by forces outside Kepler entirely [S-031]. We therefore refuse a point forecast, which would pretend to a precision we do not have, and instead model the domain as a tree of grid-price paths, each path mapped to a margin outcome [S-031].

mechanisms.body:
The tree branches on grid-price path → renewables mix → net domain contribution margin, and the headline is not a forecast but a distribution: the **probability-weighted expected margin across the whole tree is +$0.036/kWh** [S-211]. The decisive feature, though, is the shape rather than the average — five of six branches are margin-positive [S-211]. The single negative branch, *Squeeze* — in which grid prices rise and our PPAs reprice against us — carries only ~6% weight, and it carries so little precisely because the long-dated PPA book and the storage hedge (Mechanism 2.2) are the exact instruments built to collapse it [S-211]. The tail that would sink a standalone charging business is the one we have already bought insurance against.

The strategically important reading is the second-order one [S-212]. The two *Grid rises* branches are painful for standalone margin, yet they are actively helpful for the cost-advantage spread: when retail power spikes, our hedged landed cost spikes far less, so the gap we hand to Mobility (D1) and Logistics (D3) actually widens [S-212]. The domain's cross-domain value is therefore **counter-cyclical to its own P&L** — the branches that hurt the standalone Energy business are the same ones that most help the flywheel [S-212]. That is why the tree matters strategically and not merely financially: read as a silo, a rising grid is a threat; read as a feeder, it is when the moat term Energy owns pays out hardest to the domains it supplies [S-212].

The tree is not a fate we merely await; the first three drivers are the instruments that **bias us toward its upper branches** [S-033]. Data-led siting (1.1) concentrates the estate where renewables and storage are scaling fastest, tilting the renewables-mix fork in our favour before the price path is even known [S-033]. Vertical procurement (1.2) locks the PPAs that hold in the *Grid rises* branch, so the very state that punishes an unhedged operator leaves us intact [S-033]. Dispatch optimisation (2.2) is literally the mechanism that converts a *Volatile-Thin* world into a *Volatile-Win* one by arbitraging the peaks [S-033]. Reliability (3.x) ensures that whatever branch we land in, the trust output — and therefore the D4 coupling back into the flywheel — is preserved [S-033].

The property this buys is the one we most want from an infrastructure asset facing a decade of grid re-pricing: **every driver in this section is simultaneously an operating lever and a hedge on the transition tree** [S-033]. A lever in the good states, a hedge in the bad ones — the same mechanisms that raise the expected margin also compress the variance around it, which is why the tree's downside is shallow enough to leave five of six branches positive [S-033].

component_data.root: 10-year grid-price path → renewables mix → net domain contribution margin [S-211]

branches.label: Squeeze
branches.note: Grid prices rise and our PPAs reprice against us — the single negative branch of the six. Carries only ~6% weight because the long-dated PPA book and the storage hedge (Mechanism 2.2) are built precisely to collapse it. [S-211]

branches.label: Grid rises (hedged)
branches.note: Painful for standalone margin, but hedged landed cost spikes far less than retail power, so the cost gap handed to Mobility (D1) and Logistics (D3) widens — the domain's cross-domain value is counter-cyclical to its own P&L. [S-212]

branches.label: Volatile → Win
branches.note: Dispatch optimisation (Mechanism 2.2) arbitrages the peaks, converting what would be a Volatile-Thin world into a Volatile-Win one. [S-033]

branches.label: Renewables scale
branches.note: Grid decarbonises and renewables/storage scale on plan; data-led siting (1.1) has already concentrated the estate here. The majority cluster among the five margin-positive branches. [S-211]

component_data.caption: Six-branch tree; five of six branches margin-positive; probability-weighted expected margin +$0.036/kWh. Load-bearing (negative) branch Squeeze shown first. [S-211]
